steps.title: Identify what cause-and-effect requires
steps.content: To attempt to establish a cause-and-effect relationship between a treatment and an outcome, subjects must be randomly assigned to the treatment and control groups. Random assignment balances out confounding variables, so any difference in outcomes can be attributed to the treatment itself.

steps.title: Distinguish random assignment from random selection
steps.content: Random selection from the population is what lets you generalize results to that population, but it does not justify a causal claim. Random assignment to groups within the study is what makes a causal claim possible.

steps.title: Evaluate each choice
steps.content:
• The number of chipmunks in the treatment group is equal to the number of prairie dogs in the treatment group, and the number of chipmunks in the control group is equal to the number of prairie dogs in the control group.: Equal group sizes across species are not required to establish causation. Eliminated.
  • The chipmunks and the prairie dogs are randomly assigned to the treatment and control groups.: Randomly assigning the chipmunks and prairie dogs to treatment and control groups is exactly what is required for a causal conclusion.
  • The chipmunks and the prairie dogs are randomly selected from their respective habitats.: Random selection from each habitat supports generalization, not causation. Eliminated.
  • The average age of the chipmunks in the treatment group is equal to the average age of the prairie dogs in the treatment group, and the average age of the chipmunks in the control group is equal to the average age of the prairie dogs in the control group.: Matching average ages across species is not required and does not by itself enable a causal claim. Eliminated.
That matches the correct answer — B is correct.
